title: Comparative: Ministers
lead: India: ministers as ==constitutional functionaries in a written parliamentary republic==. UK: ==political-executive office holders in a convention-based constitutional monarchy==.

head: Basis
India
United Kingdom
rows: Legal basis
Royal prerogative, statutes, conventions, ==Cabinet Manual, Ministerial Code==.
Appointment & removal
PM by President; others on PM's advice; ==pleasure of the President== = PM's confidence in practice.
By the Monarch on PM's advice; hold office on ==PM's confidence==.
Types
Cabinet Ministers, MoS (independent charge), MoS, Deputy Ministers.
Cabinet, Ministers of State, ==Under-Secretaries, Lords Ministers, Whips, Law Officers==.
Collective responsibility
==Express rule — Art 75(3)==; no-confidence defeat = whole Council resigns.
==Convention== — support publicly or resign, unless explicitly suspended.
Individual responsibility
Answerable for the department; conventions ==applied unevenly==.
==Ministerial Code== — duty to Parliament to account for departments.
Membership
Must join either House ==within six months==.
Convention: member of Commons or Lords (often via Lords).
==Size limit==
Constitutional cap — ==15% of LS strength== (91st CA).
Statutory limits on paid ministers; ==no percentage cap==.
Anti-defection
Tenth-Schedule disqualification ==bars ministership== for the period.
No equivalent — rebellion managed politically.
Conduct code
Oaths, RPA norms, conflict rules — ==no single prominent code==.
Formal ==Ministerial Code== (updated June 2026).
Head-of-state relation
==Art 78==: PM must furnish information; President may require matters placed before the Council.
PM's audience with the Monarch — ==informed, consulted, warns==.
Judicial interpretation
[[Shamsher Singh]]: President/Governor act on ==aid and advice==; [[Ram Jawaya]]: executive power coextensive with legislative power.
Conventions largely political; ministerial action's ==legality== reviewable.
Character
==Constitutionalised== — text, courts, Parliament, federal structure.
==Conventionalised== — Commons confidence, Code, political norms.
